worth_it_explanation: Access to a Magnite publisher account controls pricing, deals, payment details, and reporting, so who holds which role is a direct control over revenue and the safety of the account. A role review reads the existing user list and credential setup rather than rebuilding anything, so it costs reading time, and a finding points to a concrete account someone can downgrade or remove. The same review covers the application programming interface (API) credentials that move money-adjacent data in and out of the account, where a shared or stale token carries the same risk as a stale login.
intro: Magnite is a sell-side platform (SSP), so the publisher console controls inventory pricing, demand deals, payment settings, and reporting for the inventory a publisher monetizes. This subcategory is about who can reach those controls and how programmatic access is granted, rather than the pricing or deal decisions themselves. These checks confirm that each person and each automated integration holds only the access the work requires, and that access is revoked when a person leaves or a credential is no longer needed.

audit_checks.title: User roles do not follow least privilege, and departed staff retain access
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An account with more access than the work requires widens the damage a mistake or a compromised login can do. A broad role can change pricing, alter payment details, or expose reporting that a narrower role could not. A login left active after someone leaves is an unmanaged door into a revenue-bearing account, which is why both effects raise the risk above routine hygiene.

educate.base:
Least privilege is the security principle that each person should hold the minimum access needed to do their work, and no more. The National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST) defines it as restricting access privileges to the minimum necessary to accomplish assigned tasks. In a Magnite publisher account, the controls that access reaches are not abstract. A broad role can change floor prices, edit deal terms, alter payment and banking details, and read revenue reporting, so the gap between the access a person holds and the access their work needs is a measure of avoidable risk.

The risk shows up two ways. The first is over-broad access. When an analyst who only reads reporting holds an administrator role, a single mistake or a single stolen login can change pricing or payment settings rather than just view a chart. The blast radius of any one account is set by the access that account holds, so trimming each role to its task shrinks what can go wrong. The second is access that outlives the person. When someone leaves the publisher or moves to a different team, a login that stays active is a door into a revenue-bearing account that nobody is watching, and shared logins make this worse because no single departure prompts anyone to revoke them.

A role review reads the current user list against the work each person actually does. The audit question is whether the access granted matches the task, and whether the list still contains people who have left or roles that were never scoped down after a project ended.

A few signs point to access that does not follow least privilege:
  • Users hold administrator or full-access roles when their work only requires reading reporting or managing a single function
  • Logins remain active for people who have left the publisher or moved off the account
  • Shared logins exist that more than one person uses, so no individual departure triggers a revocation
  • No periodic review of the user list is on record, so access has only ever been added, never trimmed
You may want to confirm that each active login maps to a current person whose role matches their work. One approach is to compare the user list against a current staff roster, downgrade any role broader than the task, and remove any login tied to someone who has left.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media publisher, editorial, ad operations, and finance teams often touch the same account for different reasons, so a reporter checking performance does not need the access that an ad operations lead uses to set pricing. You may want to map each role to the team's actual task, and pay particular attention to contractors and freelance staff whose access can outlast a single project.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content publisher, the people managing monetization may sit on a small team where one person wears several hats, which makes it tempting to grant everyone full access. You may want to separate the reporting-only roles from the roles that can change pricing or payment details, so a routine analytics task cannot reach the settings that move money.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's detailed documentation for managing console users and roles is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the current user list in the Magnite publisher console against a staff roster, noting each person's role and whether their access matches their work.

magnite_console.label: Magnite publisher console
steps: Open the user or account management area of the Magnite publisher console and list every active user with the role assigned to each.
For each user, note whether the role is administrator, full access, or a narrower role limited to reporting or a single function.
Compare the list against a current staff roster, and flag any login that belongs to a person who has left the publisher or moved off the account.
Flag any shared login that more than one person uses, and any role that is broader than the work that person performs.
Ask the publisher whether a periodic review of the user list is run, and record when it last happened.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active user, the role held, and whether that role matches the person's current work. An account where every login maps to a current person and each role is scoped to its task passes. An account that holds administrator roles for reporting-only work, active logins for departed staff, or shared logins is a fail recorded with the specific users and roles affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active user, the role held, and whether it matches their work above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that access to the {{site_name}} Magnite account does not follow least privilege. Some logins hold roles broader than the work requires, and at least one login remains active for a person no longer on the account. A broad role widens what a single mistake or a compromised login can change, from pricing to payment details, and a login that outlives the person is an unmanaged door into a revenue-bearing account. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the user list against a current staff roster, downgrading any role broader than the task, removing logins tied to people who have left, and replacing any shared login with named individual accounts. After the cleanup, scheduling a periodic review keeps the list from drifting back.

audit_checks.title: API credentials are shared, over-scoped, or never rotated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: An application programming interface (API) credential carries the same reach into the account as a login, so a token shared across integrations or scoped wider than its task lets one leak expose pricing, deal, and reporting data at once. A credential that is never rotated stays valid indefinitely, so a copy taken at any point keeps working, which is why scoping and rotation matter as much for tokens as for human logins.

educate.base:
Publishers often connect a Magnite account to other systems through an API, which lets a reporting dashboard, a data warehouse, or a yield tool read and write data without a person logging in. The credential that authorizes that connection, an API key or access token, carries reach into the account in the same way a login does. Treating it with less care than a human login is the gap this check looks for.

Two properties decide how much risk a credential carries. The first is scope. A token scoped to exactly the data and actions an integration needs limits what a leak can reach, while a token granted broad access lets a single leaked key expose pricing, deal, and reporting data at once. The OWASP Secrets Management guidance frames this as applying least privilege to secrets, so each credential reaches only what its job requires. The second is rotation. A credential rotated on a schedule limits how long a copy stays useful, so a key taken at some point in the past stops working once it is replaced. A credential that is never rotated stays valid indefinitely, which means a copy taken at any time keeps working until someone notices.

Shared credentials undo both properties. When one token is reused across several integrations or copied between people, no single integration can be revoked without breaking the others, and no departure or project end prompts a rotation. Tying each credential to one integration keeps scope and rotation manageable.

A few signs point to credentials that are not scoped or rotated:
  • One API credential is reused across multiple integrations rather than each integration holding its own
  • Credentials carry broad access when the integration only reads a narrow slice of reporting
  • No rotation schedule exists, so tokens created at setup are still in use unchanged
  • Credentials are stored in shared documents, chat messages, or code rather than a secrets manager
  • No record exists of which integrations hold which credentials, so a dormant key cannot be identified and removed
You may want to confirm that each integration holds its own credential, scoped to what it needs and rotated on a schedule. One approach is to inventory the active credentials, retire any that are shared or dormant, and move the rest into a secrets manager with a rotation cadence.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media publisher, the Magnite account often feeds a central reporting warehouse alongside other revenue sources, so the API credential may have been set up once and left untouched for a long time. You may want to confirm that the warehouse integration holds a read-scoped credential of its own rather than a broad token shared with other tools, and that it has been rotated since setup.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content publisher, a small team may have wired the Magnite API into a homegrown dashboard with a credential pasted directly into the code or a shared spreadsheet. You may want to move that credential into a secrets manager, scope it to the reporting the dashboard actually reads, and set a rotation schedule so a leaked copy does not stay valid forever.

educate.learn_more_note: Magnite's API authentication and credential documentation is in the partner portal (login required).

investigate.intro: Review the active API credentials tied to the Magnite account, noting which integration each belongs to, how broadly it is scoped, and when it was last rotated.

magnite_console.label: Magnite publisher console
steps: Open the API or integrations area of the Magnite publisher console and list every active API credential or access token.
For each credential, record which integration uses it, whether any other integration shares it, and the access scope it carries.
Note the creation date of each credential and ask the publisher whether a rotation schedule exists and when each was last rotated.
Ask where each credential is stored, and flag any kept in a shared document, chat message, or source code rather than a secrets manager.
Flag any credential that no current integration uses, which points to a dormant token that can be removed.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each active API credential, the integration it serves, its scope, its rotation history, and where it is stored. A setup where each integration holds its own credential, scoped to its task, rotated on a schedule, and stored in a secrets manager passes. A setup with shared credentials, broad scopes, no rotation, or credentials stored in shared documents or code is a fail recorded with the credentials and integrations affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record each active API credential, its integration, scope, rotation history, and storage above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the application programming interface (API) credentials connecting the {{site_name}} Magnite account to other systems are not scoped or rotated to limit their reach. At least one credential is shared across integrations or scoped more broadly than its task, and no rotation schedule is on record. A broad or shared credential lets one leak expose pricing, deal, and reporting data at once, and a token that is never rotated stays valid indefinitely. {{voice_recommend}} giving each integration its own credential scoped to what it needs, moving the credentials into a secrets manager, and setting a rotation schedule so a leaked copy stops working. After the change, retiring any dormant or shared credential closes the access that nothing currently relies on.
